topic: El/la articles, lo/la direct object pronouns, and no

tips.title: El and la sort nouns into two teams
tips.body: Every Spanish noun is masculine or feminine, and el/la simply tag which team it's on: el for masculine, la for feminine. Words ending in -o are usually masculine and words ending in -a are usually feminine, but there are exceptions (el día is masculine despite the -a). The safest habit is to learn each new noun together with its article.

tips.title: Lo and la can also mean 'him,' 'her,' or 'it'
tips.body: The same little words that mean 'the' can also replace a noun you already mentioned, like English 'it.' The trick is placement: in Spanish the pronoun jumps in front of the conjugated verb instead of trailing after it, so 'she knows him' becomes 'she la knows' or 'she lo knows' depending on gender.

tips.title: No always parks right before the verb
tips.body: English needs a helper word to negate ('does not know'), but Spanish just drops no directly in front of whatever verb you want to negate: 'no knows' instead of 'does not know.' It is one of the simplest rules in the whole language, and it never moves.

tips.title: Watch for the article/pronoun switch
tips.body: Because la and el can be either 'the' (in front of a noun) or 'her/him/it' (in front of a verb), it helps to ask yourself which job the word is doing in a given sentence. If a noun follows, it's an article; if a verb follows, it's standing in for something already named.

bonusCards.english: The dad found the wallet, and the mom lost it.
That is not the answer, and I know it.
The girl saw the movie, and she recommends it.
The man opened the door, and he closed it again.
bonusCards.answer: **El** man opened **la** door, **y** he **la** closed again.

bonusCards.english: I know that the boy loves the dog, and he pets it every day.
bonusCards.answer: I know **que** **el** boy loves **el** dog, **y** he **lo** pets every day.
